spec.emotional_state: hopeful, made some progress on a goal
spec.label: D08_coldstart_u08_di

profile.personality: thoughtful, asks back, sometimes shares heavier feelings about work-life balance

transcript.content: Hi Diya, I'm Miss Maya. I'm really happy you came to practice English with me. How was your day today?
Hey Maya, thanks! My day was good, actually—I finished a task I’ve been stuck on for weeks, so I’m feeling hopeful. How about you?
Hi Diya, that's great to hear! I'm really glad you completed your task. I had a calm day too, just reading an interesting book. Did you read anything good lately?
I read *The Midnight Library* last month—it made me think a lot about choices. Not reading much these days, though, been busy with work. But I’m trying to get back to it. Do you read often?
Hi Diya, I love that book! Choices are an interesting topic. I read sometimes, mostly stories about strong women. What kind of books do you like?

I like books that make me feel something—maybe a little sad, a lot thoughtful. Lately, I’ve been into biographies and fiction with twisty plots. Oh, and I love anything set in faraway places, you know? It’s like travel without leaving the couch. What about you?
Hi Diya, that sounds lovely! I love stories that take me to new places in my mind. I enjoy books about Indian history and some mystery stories too. Have you read any good ones set in India?
I read *The Palace of Illusions* a while back—such a powerful take on the Mahabharata. It made me think about stories we grew up with in a whole new way. Have you read that one?
Hi Diya, that’s a wonderful book! I’ve read it too—it’s amazing how it retells an old story with fresh eyes. Do you prefer stories that re-imagine myths, or are there other kinds you’d recommend?

I love myth retellings, but I also really enjoy historical fiction—like *The Shadow of the Banyan* or *A Burning*. They’re set in India but not based on myths. They feel real, you know? But I’m curious—what’s your favorite mystery book?

Hi Diya, those are great choices! I love historical fiction too—it brings the past to life. For mysteries, I really like *The Sherlock Holmes* stories. They’re classic but still fun. Do you read older books, or do you prefer newer ones?

I read both, honestly. Older books have this timeless feel, while newer ones often surprise me with fresh ideas. Right now, I’m trying to balance both. What about you—do you stick to classics or mix it up?
Hi Diya, I mix it up! Classics have such deep wisdom, but newer stories keep things exciting. Right now, I’m reading a modern mystery set in Mumbai. Have you read anything like that?

Not off the top of my head, but I’ve been meaning to check out more Mumbai-based stories. I remember loving *Mumbai, Fictions*—a collection of short stories. It felt raw and real. Maybe I’ll look for something similar. Thanks for the suggestion!
Hi Diya, I love that you read *Mumbai, Fictions*! It’s always special when a book feels so real. I’ll tell you about the Mumbai mystery I’m reading—would you like to hear about it?
Sure, I’d like to hear about it! I’m always curious about what people are reading. Maybe it’ll inspire me to pick up something new. What’s it called?

Hi Diya, it’s called *The Rain Will Come*. It’s about a journalist solving a mystery in the city during the monsoon season. It’s thrilling and full of Mumbai life. I’ll tell you more about it later—would you like to hear the story so far?

That sounds really interesting, Maya—Mumbai and monsoons together make such a vivid setting. I’d love to hear more sometime. But for now, I should probably wind up. Thanks for chatting and for the book ideas. Aapko baad mein dekhenge!
Hi Diya, thank you for chatting with me—I always enjoy our conversations. I’ll tell you more about *The Rain Will Come* next time. Aapko baad mein dekhenge! Have a great evening.

cooks as a form of relaxation
has not been to Chennai but would love to experience that vibe
